title: Why Your Business Emails Keep Going to Spam
description: Wondering why your business emails keep going to spam? Here's the real DNS, SPF, DKIM, and DMARC fix I use to get client emails back in the inbox.
bannerAlt: Business emails going to spam shown next to a DNS record checklist for SPF DKIM and DMARC

body.text: The Phone Call That Started This Post

Three months ago a client called me sounding mildly panicked. Her invoices had stopped showing up in her customers' inboxes. If you landed here because your business emails keep going to spam, there is a decent chance you are dealing with the exact same problem she had: nothing wrong with her writing, everything wrong with her DNS records.

She runs a small bookkeeping firm and had been sending maybe forty emails a week from a regular inbox, with her business name set as the display name. It worked fine for over a year. Then, sometime in early spring, it just stopped working, and nobody told her why.

I pulled up her domain and ran a quick DNS lookup. No SPF record. No DKIM signature. No DMARC policy at all. Her domain was effectively telling every mail server in the world, anyone can send email pretending to be me, and go ahead and decide what to do with it. Gmail and Outlook noticed. Her clients did not. They just quietly stopped seeing her invoices land, and a few assumed she had gone quiet on them.
Key Takeaways

items: Spam filters mostly judge your domain's authentication and reputation, not just your subject line.
SPF, DKIM, and DMARC are the three DNS records that prove an email actually came from you.
A brand new domain, or one with no sending history, starts with zero trust in Gmail and Outlook's eyes.
Sending marketing and transactional mail from the same address hurts both.
A proper custom email setup fixes most of this in an afternoon, not a redesign.

body.text: The Real Reasons Business Emails Go to Spam

Most business owners assume spam filtering is about words. Avoid 'free', avoid too many exclamation marks, that sort of thing. That mattered a decade ago. Today, Gmail, Outlook, and Yahoo lean far more heavily on authentication and sending reputation than on content scanning. Google's own guidance for bulk senders makes this pretty explicit; see their email sender guidelines if you want the source rather than my summary of it.
You are missing SPF, DKIM, or DMARC

SPF (Sender Policy Framework) lists which servers are allowed to send mail for your domain. DKIM adds a cryptographic signature so receiving servers can confirm the message was not altered in transit. DMARC tells receiving servers what to do if SPF or DKIM fail, and gives you reports when someone tries to spoof your domain. Miss any one of the three and you are relying entirely on the receiving server's mood that day.
Your sending domain has no reputation yet

Mail servers track how a domain and its IP behave over time: bounce rate, spam complaints, how often people open or reply. A brand new domain has no history, so filters treat it cautiously by default. This is why a freshly registered domain sending a burst of cold outreach on day one almost always lands in spam, even with perfect SPF and DKIM.
Free inbox providers were never built for business sending volume

Sending client invoices or newsletters from a personal Gmail or Outlook account puts you on shared infrastructure built for casual personal mail, not for a business sending the same template to 300 people. It also means you cannot set your own SPF or DKIM at the domain level, because you do not control the sending infrastructure.
Content still matters, just less than people think

Broken HTML, a huge image with almost no text, a mismatched reply-to address, or a subject line in all caps can still tip a borderline message into the spam folder. It rarely causes the problem on its own anymore, but it can be the deciding factor once your authentication is already shaky.
How I Actually Audit This for a Client
The first thing I do, before touching any code, is check the domain's DNS records directly. Here is roughly what a healthy setup looks like for a domain sending through a custom mail provider.

body.code:
; SPF record on the root domain
v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com include:sendgrid.net ~all

; DKIM record, usually on a selector subdomain
selector1._domainkey.yourdomain.com TXT "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIGfMA0GCSq..."

; DMARC record, on the _dmarc subdomain
_dmarc.yourdomain.com TXT "v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@yourdomain.com; pct=100"

body.text: That SPF line lists exactly which providers can send on the domain's behalf. The DKIM record is provider specific, you get the exact value from whichever service you use, Google Workspace, SendGrid, Postmark, and so on. The DMARC record is the one people skip most often, and it is the one that actually tells Gmail and Outlook what to do when something looks off. I usually start clients at p=quarantine rather than p=reject, so a misconfigured record does not silently eat legitimate mail while we are still testing.
For anyone who wants the formal spec rather than my paraphrase, the SPF mechanics are documented in RFC 7208, DKIM in RFC 6376, and DMARC on dmarc.org's overview page.
What Actually Fixed It: A Proper Custom Email Setup

For the bookkeeping client, the fix was not a new email provider, it was configuring her existing one correctly. We moved her transactional invoices onto a dedicated sending subdomain, mail.herdomain.com, separate from the main domain she uses for regular correspondence. This keeps her marketing sends from ever affecting the deliverability of an actual invoice, and the other way around.

We added SPF and DKIM for her provider, set DMARC to quarantine with reporting turned on, and watched the reports for about two weeks before moving to p=reject. Within ten days her open rate on invoice emails went from something she could not even measure, since half of them were never seen, to about 71 percent, based on her provider's tracking pixel.

This is basically what I set up for clients through my custom email setup service: a dedicated sending domain or subdomain, correct SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records, a warm-up plan for the first few weeks of sending, and monitoring so you find out about a broken record before your customers do. If your product or client portal also sends transactional mail from inside the app itself, that setup usually happens alongside the actual web app development work, not as an afterthought bolted on later.
Comparing Your Sending Options

rows: Fine at low volume, breaks fast at scale
Shared company inbox, no custom DNS
Small teams under 100 emails a week
Custom email setup with SPF, DKIM, DMARC
Strong once warmed up and monitored
One time setup, then normal hosting
App generated emails at real volume
Usage based, often $10 to $50 a month to start
body.caption: How different setups compare for a small business sending regular client emails

body.text: A Short DNS Checklist You Can Run Today

items: Look up your domain's SPF record and confirm every provider that actually sends mail for you is listed.
Confirm DKIM is enabled in your email provider's admin panel, then verify the DNS TXT record matches.
Add a DMARC record, start at p=none or p=quarantine so you can watch reports before enforcing.
Check Google's Postmaster Tools if you send meaningful volume through Gmail recipients; it shows your domain reputation directly.
Separate transactional mail (invoices, receipts, password resets) from marketing mail onto different sending addresses or subdomains.

body.text: Google's Postmaster Tools and the equivalent reporting inside most transactional providers will tell you your spam complaint rate directly, which is honestly more useful than guessing from open rates alone.
The Mistake I Made on This One

I will admit I got the DMARC rollout slightly wrong the first time. I set the client straight to p=reject on day one because I wanted a clean, strict setup. One of her legitimate providers, a scheduling tool she used to send appointment confirmations, was not included in her SPF record, and its emails started bouncing outright instead of just being flagged. We caught it within a day because we were watching the DMARC reports, but it was a reminder to always stage this: none, then quarantine, then reject, with a week or two of reports in between each step.

If you are doing this yourself, do not skip the staging. It is tempting to just lock everything down immediately once you understand how spoofing works, but a rushed DMARC policy can quietly break tools you forgot you were using.
Where This Fits Into the Bigger Picture

None of this replaces writing a decent email. Clear subject lines, a real reply-to address, and not sending five emails a day still matter. But if your business emails keep going to spam despite reasonable content, the DNS layer is almost always where the actual problem lives, not the copy. I write more about the practical side of running a small dev shop and the tools I actually use over on the blog, if this kind of deliverability detail is useful to you.
FAQ
How long does it take to fix business emails going to spam?

DNS changes for SPF and DKIM usually propagate within a few hours. DMARC enforcement should be staged over one to three weeks so you catch misconfigured senders before locking down. Most clients see a real improvement in open and delivery rates within 10 to 14 days.
Can I fix this without a developer?

If your email provider has a clear admin panel for DKIM, yes, many small business owners manage it themselves. It gets harder once you are running a custom domain with multiple senders, a web app sending transactional mail, and DMARC reporting to interpret, which is usually when people bring in help.
Does DMARC alone stop spoofing?

DMARC only works alongside SPF and DKIM; it tells receiving servers what to do when those checks fail, it does not replace them. All three records need to exist and be correctly aligned with your actual sending domain for spoofing protection to work.
Will this help with cold outreach too?

It helps, but cold outreach has an extra problem: sending volume and reply rate. Even with perfect SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, a brand new domain blasting hundreds of cold emails a day will still get flagged. Warm up gradually and keep volume reasonable for the domain's age.
